engineGuide.h2: Mitsubishi Lancer Engine Codes - Full Technical Specifications & Replacement Guide
engineGuide.h3: Every engine code fitted to the Mitsubishi Lancer across all generations, with technical specs, compatible variants and trims, known failure points, and average rebuilt prices from UK specialists. Select any engine code to compare quotes.

entries.title: 2.0L Petrol Turbo
entries.history: Legendary turbocharged unit powering Evo IV-IX; revered for tuning potential but plagued by crankwalk on 7-bolt blocks.
compatibleVariants: Evolution IV, Evolution V, Evolution VI, Evolution VII, Evolution VIII, Evolution IX, Tommi Makinen Edition
commonFailures: Crankwalk (7-bolt blocks) causing thrust bearing failure at 60k-100k miles
Balance shaft bearing seizure leading to oil starvation
Turbocharger seal failure on high-mileage units
entries.cta: Get quotes for 4G63T Mitsubishi Lancer engine replacement

entries.title: 2.0L Petrol
entries.history: Naturally aspirated variant of the 4G6 family; robust but less sought after than the turbocharged Evo version.
commonFailures: Timing belt failure if not replaced at 60k miles (interference engine)
Hydraulic lash adjuster tick at higher mileage
entries.cta: Get quotes for 4G63 Mitsubishi Lancer engine replacement

entries.title: 1.8L Petrol
entries.history: High-volume civilian engine; reliable when maintained but vulnerable to interference damage if timing belt fails.
commonFailures: Timing belt snap causing valve-to-piston contact
Oil sludge buildup with extended service intervals
entries.cta: Get quotes for 4G93 Mitsubishi Lancer engine replacement

entries.title: 1.6L Petrol
entries.history: Popular entry-level engine; MIVEC variant offered variable valve timing for improved efficiency and response.
commonFailures: Hydraulic lash adjuster tick noise at idle
Coil pack failure causing misfires under load
entries.cta: Get quotes for 4G92 Mitsubishi Lancer engine replacement

entries.title: 1.5L Petrol
entries.history: Budget-focused workhorse; simple SOHC design prioritises affordability over performance.
commonFailures: Ignition coil pack failure causing rough idle
Carbon buildup on intake valves at high mileage
entries.cta: Get quotes for 4G15 Mitsubishi Lancer engine replacement

entries.title: 2.0L Petrol Turbo
entries.history: Modern aluminium-block turbo engine for Evo X and Ralliart; improved reliability over 4G63T but timing chain concerns emerged.
compatibleVariants: Evolution X, Evolution X FQ-300/330/360/400/440, Ralliart
commonFailures: Timing chain stretch and tensioner failure at 70k-100k miles
Turbo oil seal leaks on high-mileage examples
entries.cta: Get quotes for 4B11T Mitsubishi Lancer engine replacement

entries.title: 2.0L Petrol
entries.history: Naturally aspirated version of the modern MIVEC family; efficient and smooth but less common in UK Lancers.
compatibleVariants: 2.0 DI-D (diesel variant uses different code), some export markets
commonFailures: Timing chain guide wear at high mileage
Oil consumption on worn piston rings
entries.cta: Get quotes for 4B11 Mitsubishi Lancer engine replacement

entries.title: 1.8L Petrol
entries.history: Modern replacement for the 4G93; lighter aluminium construction with MIVEC variable timing for efficiency.
compatibleVariants: 1.8 MIVEC, Sportback 1.8, GS3, GS4, SE, SGX
commonFailures: Oil consumption due to piston ring wear
Timing chain tensioner rattle on cold start
entries.cta: Get quotes for 4B10 Mitsubishi Lancer engine replacement

entries.title: 1.5L Petrol
entries.history: Entry-level modern engine co-developed with Daimler; compact design prioritises fuel economy for daily driving.
commonFailures: Timing chain stretch causing rattling noise
VVT actuator failure leading to poor idle
entries.cta: Get quotes for 4A91 Mitsubishi Lancer engine replacement

entries.title: 1.6L Petrol
entries.history: Slightly larger displacement variant of the 4A9 family; offers marginally more torque for heavier Lancer variants.
commonFailures: Timing chain guide wear at high mileage
Oil consumption on worn units
entries.cta: Get quotes for 4A92 Mitsubishi Lancer engine replacement

entries.title: 1.3L Petrol
entries.history: Smallest engine offered in the Lancer range; basic SOHC design focused on affordability and low running costs.
commonFailures: Coil pack failure causing misfires
Carburettor issues on earliest models
entries.cta: Get quotes for 4G13 Mitsubishi Lancer engine replacement

commonProblems.h2: Mitsubishi Lancer Engine Problems - What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense
commonProblems.h3: The Mitsubishi Lancer's reliability varies dramatically between its civilian 4G9/4A9 engines and the high-performance 4G63T/4B11T Evolution units. Every rebuilt engine supplied through EngineMarket includes upgraded internal components designed to address the original failure point - plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. Use the cost comparisons below to decide whether repair or replacement offers better value for your Lancer.

problems.h4: 4G63 Crankwalk Failure - Why Evolution IV-IX Engines Seize Prematurely
problems.affectedModels: Mitsubishi Lancer Evolution IV, V, VI, VII, VIII, IX (1996-2007, 4G63T 7-bolt block)
problems.rootCause: Inadequate thrust bearing design on 7-bolt crankshafts allows excessive end-play, causing crankshaft to walk forward and seize.

repairOptions.tier: Minor - Preventative Inspection - Check crank end-play before failure
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Remove gearbox, measure crank end-play, advise on risk
repairOptions.longevity: Only viable before symptoms appear; does not fix existing wear

repairOptions.tier: Full Replacement - Rebuilt 4G63T Engine - Upgraded thrust bearings
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit rebuilt unit with upgraded bearings, bench-tested, 12-month warranty
repairOptions.longevity: Addresses root cause; suitable for cars worth £3k+ or kept long-term

problems.recommendation: On an Evolution worth £4,000-£8,000, preventative inspection is wise at 50k miles. Once crankwalk symptoms appear (clutch pedal stuck, knocking), replacement is the only viable option - repair costs approach replacement value.
problems.cta: Compare rebuilt 4G63T Evolution engine prices for your Mitsubishi Lancer

problems.h4: 4B11 Timing Chain Stretch - Why Evo X and Ralliart Engines Rattle
problems.rootCause: Timing chain tensioner wear allows chain slack, causing rattling noise and potential valve timing drift or jump.

repairOptions.tier: Intermediate - Chain & Tensioner Replacement - Before catastrophic failure
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace timing chain, tensioner, guides; reset timing
repairOptions.longevity: Viable if caught early before valve contact; adds 40k-60k miles

repairOptions.tier: Full Replacement - Rebuilt 4B11T Engine - Upgraded tensioner design
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit rebuilt unit with revised tensioner, bench-tested, 12-month warranty
repairOptions.longevity: Best for high-mileage Evos or cars kept long-term; eliminates recurrence risk

problems.recommendation: If you hear cold-start rattle but no performance loss, chain replacement offers good value. Once timing has jumped or valves are bent, only full replacement makes financial sense.
problems.cta: Get quotes for 4B11T Mitsubishi Lancer engine replacement

problems.h4: 4G93 Timing Belt Failure - Why 1.8 GLS Engines Suffer Catastrophic Damage
problems.rootCause: Interference design means snapped timing belt causes pistons to strike open valves, bending valves and damaging pistons.

repairOptions.tier: Minor - Preventative Belt Replacement - Before failure occurs
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace timing belt, tensioner, water pump; reset timing
repairOptions.longevity: Essential maintenance; prevents catastrophic damage if done at 60k miles

repairOptions.tier: Full Replacement - Rebuilt 4G93 Engine - After belt failure
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit rebuilt unit, bench-tested, 12-month warranty
repairOptions.longevity: Only option once valves are bent; cost-effective vs cylinder head repair

problems.recommendation: Never skip the 60k-mile belt change on a 4G93. If the belt has snapped, a rebuilt engine is almost always cheaper than repairing bent valves and damaged pistons.
problems.cta: Compare rebuilt 4G93 Mitsubishi Lancer engine prices

problems.h4: Turbocharger Failure - Why High-Mileage Evolution Engines Lose Boost
problems.affectedModels: Mitsubishi Lancer Evolution IV-IX (4G63T), Evolution X/Ralliart (4B11T)
problems.rootCause: Turbo oil seals wear, allowing oil into intake/exhaust; bearing wear causes shaft play and loss of boost pressure.

repairOptions.tier: Intermediate - Turbo Rebuild/Replacement - Isolated turbo failure
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Remove old turbo, fit refurbished or new unit, reset ECU adaptations
repairOptions.longevity: Viable if engine internals are clean; risk of metal contamination from failed turbo

repairOptions.tier: Full Replacement - Rebuilt Turbo Engine - After metal contamination
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit rebuilt engine with new turbo, flushed oil system, 12-month warranty
repairOptions.longevity: Required if turbo failure sent metal into engine; prevents repeat failure

problems.recommendation: If turbo failed suddenly with no prior warning signs and the engine still runs smoothly, a turbo-only replacement may suffice. If you see metal in the oil or hear bottom-end knocking, full engine replacement is the only safe option.
problems.cta: Find rebuilt turbo Mitsubishi Lancer Evolution engines

problems.h4: Hydraulic Lash Adjuster Tick - Why 4G92/4G15 Engines Develop Valve Noise
problems.rootCause: Hydraulic lifters lose ability to maintain zero lash due to oil passage clogging or internal wear, causing ticking noise.

repairOptions.tier: Minor - Lifter Cleaning/Replacement - Address noise only
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Remove valve cover, clean or replace faulty lifters, reset valve clearance
repairOptions.longevity: Addresses symptom only; suitable if engine otherwise healthy and noise is only issue

repairOptions.tier: Full Replacement - Rebuilt Engine - If noise indicates broader wear
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit rebuilt unit with new lifters, bench-tested, 12-month warranty
repairOptions.longevity: Recommended if tick is accompanied by oil consumption or performance loss

problems.recommendation: A pure lifter tick with no other symptoms is often cosmetic. However, if the noise is accompanied by oil burning or power loss, the lifter failure may signal broader internal wear - replacement then offers better long-term value.
problems.cta: Check used 4G92 Mitsubishi Lancer engine availability

finalCta.h4: Don't Let Engine Failure Write Off Your Mitsubishi Lancer
finalCta.paragraph: Whether you own a budget 1.5 GL or a flagship Evolution X, engine failure doesn't have to mean the end of your Lancer. EngineMarket makes it simple to compare prices from trusted UK Mitsubishi Lancer specialists who supply rebuilt, reconditioned and used engines with upgraded components addressing known failure points. Every unit includes a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty and nationwide delivery options. Compare quotes today and get back on the road without paying main dealer premiums.
finalCta.buttonText: Compare Mitsubishi Lancer Engine Replacement Prices Now →
finalCta.disclaimer: All prices are indicative UK market ranges based on historical quote data and repair requests from our network of engine specialists. Dealer pricing is estimated based on published main dealer labour rates and genuine OEM parts pricing. Specialist pricing reflects actual quote data from EngineMarket's UK network. Actual costs vary depending on damage severity, vehicle condition, variant, and workshop location within the UK. Use our free comparison tool to get tailored quotes for your specific Mitsubishi Lancer and engine code. Every engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty - always confirm exact terms with your chosen supplier.

engineSizes.h2: Mitsubishi Lancer Engines by Size - Find the Right Displacement for Your Variant
engineSizes.intro: Every displacement fitted to the Mitsubishi Lancer is listed below with compatible engine codes, production years and common failure notes to help you identify the right replacement.

items.title: Mitsubishi Lancer 1.3L Petrol Engines
items.description: The smallest petrol option in the Lancer range, the 1.3L 4G13 powered entry-level models from the 1996-2002 generation. Simple SOHC design prioritised affordability and low running costs over performance, making it a common choice for first-time Lancer owners and fleet use.
commonFailurePoints: Coil pack failure causing misfires; carburettor issues on earliest models
items.cta: Compare Mitsubishi Lancer 1.3L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mitsubishi Lancer 1.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: A high-volume displacement across multiple Lancer generations, the 1.5L appeared as the 4G15 in early models and the modern 4A91 MIVEC from 2008. Offers a practical balance of fuel economy and adequate performance for daily driving.
commonFailurePoints: Ignition coil failure; timing chain stretch on 4A91 units at high mileage
items.cta: Compare Mitsubishi Lancer 1.5L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mitsubishi Lancer 1.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.6L displacement featured the popular 4G92 engine in 1996-2002 models and the later 4A92 MIVEC. The 4G92 MIVEC variant offered variable valve timing for improved responsiveness, making it a favourite among enthusiast owners of the era.
commonFailurePoints: Hydraulic lash adjuster tick; coil pack failure under load
items.cta: Compare Mitsubishi Lancer 1.6L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mitsubishi Lancer 1.8L Petrol Engines
items.description: A versatile displacement appearing as the robust 4G93 in early Lancers and the modern aluminium-block 4B10 from 2008. The 1.8L offered strong mid-range torque for comfortable motorway cruising and was commonly specified on higher-trim GLS and Sportback models.
commonFailurePoints: Timing belt failure on 4G93 (interference); oil consumption on high-mileage 4B10
items.cta: Compare Mitsubishi Lancer 1.8L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mitsubishi Lancer 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The flagship displacement for the Lancer, appearing as the legendary turbocharged 4G63T in Evolution IV-IX and the modern 4B11/4B11T in Evolution X and Ralliart. Also available in naturally aspirated form for certain export markets. Demands specialist knowledge for replacement due to performance tuning and known failure modes.
commonFailurePoints: 4G63 crankwalk (7-bolt); 4B11 timing chain stretch; turbo seal failure on high-mileage units
items.cta: Get quotes for Mitsubishi Lancer 2.0L petrol engines

items.title: Mitsubishi Lancer Petrol Engines
items.description: Petrol engines dominated the Lancer range across all generations, from economical 1.3L units to the legendary 2.0L turbocharged Evolution powerplants.
items.descriptor: Petrol engines dominated the Lancer range across all generations, from economical 1.3L units to the legendary 2.0L turbocharged Evolution powerplants.
items.familiesLabel: Common Mitsubishi Lancer Petrol Engine Families
families: 4G63T - Evolution IV-IX turbo; crankwalk risk on 7-bolt blocks, high replacement demand
4B11T - Evolution X/Ralliart turbo; timing chain stretch concerns at high mileage
4G93 - 1.8L civilian workhorse; interference engine, timing belt critical
4A91 - Modern 1.5L MIVEC; timing chain and VVT actuator issues reported
knownFor: Evolution 4G63T legendary tuning potential but crankwalk vulnerability
Civilian 4G9/4A9 engines reliable with timely maintenance
Timing components (belt/chain) critical failure points across all families
High UK replacement demand on both budget civilian and performance Evolution variants
typicalModels: Evolution IV, Evolution VI, Evolution IX, Evolution X

items.title: Mitsubishi Lancer Diesel Engines
items.description: Diesel Lancers were offered in limited numbers in the UK, primarily the 1.8 DI-D and 2.0 DI-D variants from the 2008-2017 generation.
items.descriptor: Diesel Lancers were offered in limited numbers in the UK, primarily the 1.8 DI-D and 2.0 DI-D variants from the 2008-2017 generation.

families: 4N13 - 1.8L DI-D turbo diesel; DPF and EGR issues on short-journey cars
4N14 - 2.0L DI-D turbo diesel; higher torque for heavier variants

DPF regeneration issues if used primarily for short trips
Lower replacement demand than petrol variants in UK market

engineYears.h2: Mitsubishi Lancer Engines by Year - What Was Fitted & When
engineYears.intro: This technical timeline covers all major engine changes across the Mitsubishi Lancer's UK production history, organised so owners can quickly identify their engine and compare rebuilt prices from specialists.

years.description: The fifth-generation Lancer launched with a diverse petrol lineup, headlined by the debut of the turbocharged 4G63T in the Evolution IV.
keyChanges: Introduction of Evolution IV with 4G63T turbo engine (1996)
Tommi Makinen Edition Evolution VI launched (1999)
knownFor: 4G63T established legendary Evo reputation but introduced crankwalk risk
4G93 timing belt critical - interference engine design
High UK registration numbers for civilian 1.6/1.8 variants
Strong enthusiast community support for parts and knowledge
years.cta: Get quotes for 1996-2002 Mitsubishi Lancer replacement engines
years.ticker: Mitsubishi Lancer Evolution engine replacement • 4G93 timing belt failure • 1.8 GLS engine cost UK

years.description: The sixth generation saw the Evolution continue with refined 4G63T while civilian models received updated 4G18 and carry-over 4G9 engines.
keyChanges: Civilian CS-chassis Lancer used 4G18 1.6L and carry-over 4G9 engines
Final year of 4G63T production before 4B11 introduction (2007)
knownFor: Evolution IX widely considered peak of 4G63T development
7-bolt crankwalk issue became widely recognised in enthusiast circles
Civilian CS chassis less common in UK than Gen 5 or Gen 7
years.cta: Get quotes for 2003-2007 Mitsubishi Lancer replacement engines
years.ticker: Evolution IX engine replacement • 4G63 crankwalk fix • CS chassis engine compatibility

years.description: The final Lancer generation introduced modern aluminium-block engines (4A9/4B1 families) and the all-new 4B11T turbo for Evolution X.
keyChanges: Evolution X launched with new 4B11T turbo engine (2007)
Civilian models switched to 4A91/4A92/4B10 MIVEC engines
Ralliart variant offered detuned 4B11T as performance alternative
Production ended in 2017; no direct successor in most markets
knownFor: 4B11T improved on 4G63T but introduced timing chain concerns
Modern MIVEC engines efficient but timing chain service critical
High demand for used Evolution X engines as values rise
Diesel variants rare in UK; petrol dominates replacement enquiries
years.ticker: Evolution X 4B11T replacement • 4A91 timing chain rattle • Sportback 1.8 engine cost

engineYears.closing: Not sure which year your Mitsubishi Lancer was built? Enter your registration number and we'll identify the exact engine, model year, and compatible replacement options instantly - all backed by a minimum 12‑month warranty from UK Mitsubishi Lancer specialists.

faq.h2: Mitsubishi Lancer Engine Replacement - Frequently Asked Questions

items.question: What engines fit a Mitsubishi Lancer?
items.answer: Only engines officially fitted to your specific Lancer generation and variant are guaranteed compatible. The 4G63T (Evolution IV-IX) will not swap into a civilian 4G93 Lancer without major modification.
keyPoints: Engine codes must match your chassis generation (CJ/CK, CS, or CY/CZ)
ECU, wiring harness and mounts differ significantly between variants
Use your registration to verify exact compatible engine codes instantly
Our specialists confirm fitment before dispatching any replacement unit
items.cta: Check compatible Mitsubishi Lancer engine codes for your variant

items.question: How much does it cost to replace a Mitsubishi Lancer engine?
items.answer: Rebuilt Mitsubishi Lancer engine prices range from £750 for a basic 1.3L 4G13 to £8,200 for a high-spec Evolution X 4B11T, depending on engine code, variant and condition type selected.
keyPoints: Civilian 1.5-1.8L petrol engines: £800-£2,600 rebuilt supply-only
Evolution 4G63T engines: £2,800-£7,500 rebuilt supply-only
Evolution X 4B11T engines: £3,500-£8,200 rebuilt supply-only
Supply & fit labour adds £450-£1,200 depending on complexity
items.cta: Compare rebuilt Mitsubishi Lancer engine prices for your budget

items.question: Is the 4G63 Evolution engine reliable?
items.answer: The 4G63T is robust when maintained but has two critical weaknesses: crankwalk on 7-bolt blocks (1999-2007) and balance shaft bearing failure, both potentially catastrophic if not addressed.
keyPoints: Crankwalk risk highest on 7-bolt 4G63T engines (Evolution VII-IX)
Regular oil changes with correct spec oil are critical for longevity
Rebuilt units from our network include upgraded thrust bearings
Expect 60k-100k mile service life with proper maintenance and driving
items.cta: Get quotes for upgraded 4G63T Mitsubishi Lancer Evolution engines

items.question: What causes 4B11 timing chain failure in the Evolution X?
items.answer: Timing chain stretch and tensioner wear on the 4B11/4B11T can cause rattling on cold start and, if ignored, may lead to timing jump and valve damage at 70k-110k miles.
keyPoints: Listen for rattling noise on cold startup as early warning sign
Chain replacement costs £1,100-£2,100 if caught before damage
Rebuilt 4B11T engines include revised tensioner design to prevent recurrence
Regular oil changes with correct viscosity extend chain life significantly
items.cta: Find rebuilt 4B11T Mitsubishi Lancer Evolution X engines with warranty

items.question: Can I fit a Lancer engine myself?
items.answer: While mechanically possible for experienced DIYers, Lancer engine replacement requires specialist tools, ECU coding capability and post-installation testing that most home garages lack.
keyPoints: Evolution turbo engines require ECU adaptation after installation
Incorrect torque specs or timing can cause immediate catastrophic failure
Our supply & fit option includes warranty-validating professional installation
DIY installs may void the 12-month warranty on rebuilt engines
items.cta: Book Mitsubishi Lancer engine supply & fit with warranty protection

items.question: Do you warranty Mitsubishi Lancer Evolution engines?
items.answer: Yes, every rebuilt or reconditioned Mitsubishi Lancer engine supplied through EngineMarket includes a minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty, including high-performance Evolution units.
keyPoints: Warranty covers parts and labour for manufacturing defects
Evolution engines may qualify for extended warranty options from select specialists
Keep service records and use correct oil spec to maintain warranty validity
Warranty is transferable if you sell the vehicle within the coverage period
items.cta: Compare Mitsubishi Lancer Evolution engine warranty terms from UK specialists

items.question: Are used Mitsubishi Lancer engines reliable?
items.answer: Used Lancer engines can offer excellent value if sourced from low-mileage, accident-damaged donors with full service history, but require careful vetting to avoid hidden issues.
keyPoints: Always request compression test results and service history before purchase
Civilian 4G9/4A9 engines under 80k miles offer best used-engine value
Avoid used Evolution engines without documented maintenance and mileage
Our network vets all donor vehicles; ask for history reports before committing
items.cta: Check vetted used Mitsubishi Lancer engine availability today

items.question: How long does a Mitsubishi Lancer engine last?
items.answer: With proper maintenance, civilian 4G9/4A9 Lancer engines commonly reach 150k-200k miles, while Evolution 4G63T/4B11T units typically last 80k-120k miles before major service or replacement.
keyPoints: Oil change intervals and correct oil spec are critical for longevity
Timing component replacement at manufacturer intervals prevents catastrophic failure
Evolution engines subjected to hard driving or tuning may require earlier replacement
Rebuilt engines with upgraded components often exceed original service life
items.cta: Get quotes for long-life rebuilt Mitsubishi Lancer engines

items.question: What's the difference between reconditioned and rebuilt Lancer engines?
items.answer: Reconditioned engines receive cleaning, inspection and replacement of obvious wear items, while rebuilt engines undergo comprehensive teardown with upgraded components targeting known failure points.
keyPoints: Rebuilt engines include upgrades for model-specific weaknesses (e.g., crankwalk fix)
Reconditioned units offer good value for low-stress civilian Lancer applications
Both options include minimum 12-month warranty when sourced through our network
Choose rebuilt for Evolution or high-mileage civilian engines; reconditioned for budget daily drivers
items.cta: Compare reconditioned vs rebuilt Mitsubishi Lancer engine options

Enter your registration to receive tailored quotes matching your exact engine code

No obligation to accept any quote; compare at your own pace
